blocks.text: Chapter 6.
Diminishing Prejudice: Rethinking Us vs. Them
The ideas in 30 seconds

Group hostility rarely comes from deep ideological differences; rivalries form predictably wherever groups are geographically close, evenly matched, and compete repeatedly — which means the hatred is largely an accident of circumstance.
Stereotypes survive because we hold them about people we never actually meet, and because talking mostly to our own group polarizes our beliefs further rather than testing them.

Counterfactual thinking — seriously asking "what if I'd been born on the other side?" — destabilizes prejudice more effectively than positive propaganda about the out-group, because it exposes how arbitrary our group memberships are.

People abandon prejudice when they discover the absurdity of their beliefs themselves; Daryl Davis helped some 200 KKK members leave the Klan not by arguing but by asking questions and letting contradictions surface.
Broadening an identity (from "Manchester United fan" to "football fan") expands who counts as "us" and measurably increases helping behavior toward former rivals.

Dialogue with bigots can work, but the burden of that labor should never be assigned to the targets of the bigotry; rethinking prejudice is primarily the responsibility of those who hold it and their peers.
Rivalries Are Manufactured, Not Inevitable

Yankees and Red Sox fans provide a clean laboratory for prejudice because the stakes are objectively trivial yet the hostility is real. In surveys, hardcore fans admit they'd refuse small kindnesses to rival supporters, and some say they'd actively harm them in minor ways — passing up money, for instance, if it meant a rival fan lost out too.

Neuroscientist Mina Cikara has shown this runs deeper than trash talk. When die-hard fans watch their rival fail — even against a neutral third team — reward circuitry in the brain lights up. This pleasure in another group's suffering, schadenfreude, predicts real behavior: the more activation, the more willing fans are to heckle, insult, or deny help to rival supporters. Hating the rival becomes part of the pleasure of loving your own team.

Where do rivalries come from? Research by Gavin Kilduff finds they emerge from three mundane ingredients: proximity (teams or groups near each other), repeated competition (frequent head-to-head contests), and evenly matched records (close outcomes raise the stakes). None of these involves the other side actually being bad. That's the first crack in the wall: if rivalry is produced by geography and scheduling, the moral contempt attached to it can't be justified by anything the rivals have done.

This is social identity at work. We define ourselves partly through groups, and clarity about who we are often comes cheapest from clarity about who we're not. Identifying as a fan gives belonging and meaning; identifying against another group sharpens the boundary. The same machinery that binds Sox fans against the Yankees underlies far darker divisions — which is why a baseball rivalry is a useful, low-stakes place to study how prejudice forms and how it can be unwound.
Why Stereotypes Don't Update Themselves

Stereotypes persist through a self-sealing loop. First, we typically form beliefs about rival groups without knowing many of their members — the caricature fills the vacuum left by absent contact. Second, we spend our time with people who share our view, and group polarization kicks in: interacting mainly with the like-minded doesn't moderate beliefs, it makes them more extreme, as each conversation supplies fresh confirming arguments and social rewards for zeal.

Third, when a stereotype does meet a disconfirming case — a genuinely kind Yankees fan — we protect the belief by subtyping: that person becomes "one of the good ones," an exception that leaves the rule untouched. This is why simply showcasing admirable members of a disliked group is a weak tool for changing minds. Grant tested exactly this with baseball fans: prompting them to reflect on positive qualities of rival fans did little, because the fans quarantined the counterexamples.

The elasticity of "us" was demonstrated in Mark Levine's field experiment with Manchester United supporters. A jogger (a confederate) slipped and fell in front of them. When he wore a Manchester United shirt, the vast majority stopped to help; in a Liverpool shirt or a plain shirt, only about a third did. But when researchers first asked the fans to write about what they loved about being football fans — a broader identity — helping rates for the Liverpool-shirted runner jumped to roughly 70 percent. The stranger hadn't changed; the category had. Whom we help depends less on their group than on how widely we've drawn the circle of our own.
The lesson: prejudice usually won't erode through exposure to good examples or through more time inside our own echo chambers. It erodes when something destabilizes the categories themselves.
The Counterfactual Question: What If You'd Been Born There?

Grant ran a series of experiments with Yankees and Red Sox fans to find what actually loosens hostility. The winning intervention was counterfactual thinking: asking fans to seriously consider how their loyalties would differ under slightly different life circumstances. What if you'd been born in the other city? Raised by a family that worshipped the other team? Nearly everyone concedes they'd be cheering for the enemy — which quietly concedes that their current allegiance reflects birthplace, not virtue, and the rival's allegiance reflects no vice.

A companion approach worked too: prompting fans to reflect on the arbitrariness of animosity itself — recognizing that the rivalry exists because of proximity and schedules, not because rival fans are worse people. Fans who did either kind of reflection became measurably less hostile: less willing to insult or sabotage rival supporters, more willing to help them.

Two mechanisms make this effective. First, counterfactuals don't attack the belief head-on, so there's no position to defend; the person generates the destabilizing insight themselves, which is far more persuasive than receiving it (the same principle behind motivational interviewing, covered in chapter 5). Second, the reflection targets the foundation of the stereotype — the assumption that group membership is meaningful and deserved — rather than its content. Once you see your own group identity as an accident, contempt for the other side becomes hard to sustain.

One caution from the research: uniting rivals against a common enemy (Sox and Yankees fans bonding over hating a third team) is a false shortcut. It doesn't reduce prejudice; it just relocates it, reinforcing the underlying us-versus-them habit. Durable change requires seeing through the habit, not redirecting it.
Daryl Davis: Dismantling Hate One Conversation at a Time

Daryl Davis, a Black blues and boogie-woogie musician, has spent decades befriending members of the Ku Klux Klan. It began when a white audience member, astonished that a Black pianist could play like Jerry Lee Lewis, bought him a drink and admitted he was a Klansman. Davis's driving question — "How can you hate me when you don't even know me?" — became a method.

The method looks nothing like debate. Davis seeks out Klansmen, listens to their views at length without interrupting, asks genuine questions, and lets them encounter the contradiction between their stereotypes and the man sitting across from them. He doesn't set out to convert anyone; he sets out to understand. The rethinking happens on the other side of the table, at the other person's pace. It's the interpersonal version of the counterfactual experiments: contact plus curiosity destabilizes a category the believer thought was solid.

The results are remarkable. Roughly 200 Klan members have renounced the organization through relationships with Davis, many handing him their robes and hoods, which he keeps as a record. Among them was Roger Kelly, an Imperial Wizard of the Maryland Klan, who rose within the organization even as his friendship with Davis deepened — then eventually quit, and later asked Davis to be his daughter's godfather.

Davis's story confirms the chapter's core claim at maximum difficulty: even identities built entirely on hatred can be rethought, and the lever is not superior arguments but sustained, curious, non-defensive contact that lets people discover the absurdity of their own beliefs.
The Burden of Dialogue Shouldn't Fall on the Targets

Davis's example comes with an essential caveat that Grant is careful to flag. Davis chose this work; nothing about his success implies that Black Americans — or any group facing prejudice — are obligated to sit with their haters and patiently educate them. Expecting victims to shoulder the emotional labor of deradicalizing their oppressors adds a second injustice on top of the first.

The primary responsibility for rethinking prejudice belongs to the people who hold it. The secondary responsibility belongs to their in-group peers — the people bigots will actually listen to without defensiveness, because shared identity keeps the conversation from feeling like an attack. A white friend challenging a racist joke, a fellow fan questioning the vitriol, a co-member asking "would you believe this if you'd been born elsewhere?" can reach places an outsider cannot.

This division of labor follows directly from the psychology. Messages from out-group members trigger identity defense; the same message from an in-group member gets processed as information. So while one-on-one contact across group lines can transform individuals, the scalable work of diminishing prejudice runs through insiders willing to introduce doubt into their own communities — and through structures (like the counterfactual prompts) that help people question categories without anyone having to volunteer as a target.

The unifying thread of the chapter: prejudice is not defeated by proving the other group is good. It's defeated by helping people notice that the wall between the groups was built by accident — and that they would have been standing on the other side of it had the accident gone differently.
In practice
Pick a group you feel reflexive hostility toward and write out the counterfactual honestly: if you'd been born in their town, family, or circumstances, what would you believe? Sit with the answer.

When you catch yourself explaining a rivalry by the other side's character, list the situational ingredients instead — proximity, repeated competition, close contests — and ask what the contempt is actually based on.
Notice subtyping in real time: when someone violates your stereotype of their group, resist filing them as "an exception" and instead treat them as evidence the category itself is unreliable.
Broaden the identity frame before a tense encounter: consciously name a category that includes both of you (parents, engineers, fans of the sport itself) and act from that circle.

If you want to change a prejudiced person's mind, borrow Davis's method — ask sincere questions, listen without interrupting, and let them articulate their own contradictions rather than prosecuting them.
If you belong to the same group as someone voicing prejudice, speak up — you are the messenger they can hear without defensiveness. Never demand that the targets of the prejudice do this work.
